sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presented with a non-healing wound on his right foot, approximately 2-3 weeks in duration.
He reports no trauma to the foot and initially used a band-aid to manage the wound.
He had previously seen a podiatrist but was unable to continue care due to the podiatrist's relocation.
He had been treated with antibiotics by his primary care physician, which he completed the day before the visit.
He denies pain or discomfort in the wound, but notes that it sticks to his sock when he removes it.
He also reports neuropathy and has been wearing new shoes that caused a blister, which eventually became the current wound.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with a last HbA1c of slightly over 8.
He does not mention any other past medical conditions or treatments.
Review of systems: The patient denies fever, chills, drainage from the wound, or any other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.
Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, and no necrosis.
There is no odor, and no bony exposure.
The vascular exam reveals palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, with no palpable DP or PT pulses, but with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer on the right foot, with a need for further evaluation and management to promote wound healing.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Suspected poor blood supply to the foot; Uncontrolled diabetes.
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an ankle brachial index (ABI) to assess the blood supply to the foot.
I will also have the patient wear a surgical shoe to offload pressure from the area.
I do not think further antibiotics are needed at this time.
I will refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply to the foot.
I will see the patient again in two weeks.
I will also refer the patient to a diabetic specialist to discuss ways to control their diabetes and reduce their HbA1c.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in two weeks to reassess the wound and discuss further management.
